title: ‘Let’s call it earth grief’: Naming pain and loss in the age of climate crisis
content: For decades, climate professionals and the informed public have been living with the disturbing knowledge of what is coming. Now, as the impacts of climate predictions begin to come true, we live with the devastating reality of what is happening, and we feel it deeply. This sorrow deserves a name.

creator: Stephanie Klotz
sourceDescription: Insight and inspiration in turbulent times.

title: A brief introduction to the bioregional movement and bioregional congressing
content: As climate disruption, biodiversity loss and political polarization deepen, a revived bioregional movement is emerging worldwide. This piece traces its roots and explores why place is becoming a source of resilience and belonging.

creator: Karie Crisp

title: Food waste isn’t just about what we throw away, it’s a systems problem
content: Across discussions on policy, technology, supply chains, and community partnerships at a food waste summit earlier this year, a different pattern emerged: food waste rarely appeared as an isolated issue. Instead, it surfaced as a signal of deeper system failures.

creator: Beverly Popoola

title: Growth mania is fueling dangerous advances in AI – and its existential risks
content: AI risk represents the apex of growth mania, where a few tech elites gamble with humanity’s future. They take these risks without our consent and without our interests in mind. The result could be humanity being replaced as Earth’s most intelligent species by a silicon-based successor.

creator: Dave Rollo

title: Uganda’s peasant movements push back against harmful pesticides with agroecology
content: While Europe has banned hazardous pesticides, the same chemicals are exported to African countries such as Uganda, where farmers often apply them with minimal protection and without legal recourse. ESAFF Uganda and its partners are challenging the EU and demanding change.
snippet: While Europe has banned hazardous pesticides, the same chemicals are exported to African countries such as Uganda, where farmers often apply them with minimal protection and without legal recourse.
creator: East and Southern Africa Small-scale Farmer Forum (ESAFF)
sourceDescription: Insight and inspiration in turbulent times.

title: Human Nature Odyssey, Episode 24. Stories That Create or Destroy: Myth, Death, and Animism with Sophie Strand
content: Poet and author Sophie Strand joins Human Nature Odyssey for a wide-ranging conversation about stories, illness, ecology, and transformation. Drawing from her own experience living with chronic illness, Sophie explores how the stories we inherit shape our relationship to the living world.

creator: Alex Leff

title: Musk’s trillion-dollar fortune is a flashing warning light for democracy
content: Elon Musk becoming the planet’s first trillionaire should alarm anyone worried about democracy. At that level of wealth, you’re no longer just buying another mansion or private jet; the dangerous power of concentrated wealth is to distort markets, politics, and society.

creator: Chuck Collins

title: The ripple effects of organizing against data centers
content: One city’s success in stopping a data center grew into a regional movement that’s notching wins across the San Gabriel Valley.
snippet: One city’s success in stopping a data center grew into a regional movement that’s notching wins across the San Gabriel Valley.
creator: Victoria Valenzuela

title: UN biodiversity pledge: Brazil’s strategy to protect nature and address climate change
content: The world’s most biodiverse nation, Brazil, has belatedly published its UN plan for halting and reversing nature decline by the end of this decade.
snippet: The world’s most biodiverse nation, Brazil, has belatedly published its UN plan for halting and reversing nature decline by the end of this decade.
creator: Daisy Dunne

title: Do organic farms use pesticides? How organic and conventional farming differ
content: Understanding how food is produced can help consumers make choices that support healthier soil, water, pollinators, and communities.
snippet: Understanding how food is produced can help consumers make choices that support healthier soil, water, pollinators, and communities.

sourceDescription: Insight and inspiration in turbulent times.

title: ‘Resistance is only half the equation’: Why movements must build, not just resist
content: This moment demands more than opposition. What is needed is not just resistance against corrupt centralized systems, but to create new, local systems that restructure power so it is dispersed throughout society. Because the problem is not only that those in power abuse it. The problem is that power is concentrated in the first place.

creator: Barbara Peterson
